title: Script 1: Count the Number of Used Library Cells in a Design

items.text: This script reports the count of each library cell that is actually instantiated in the current design. Unused library cells are automatically ignored.
items.html: This script reports the count of each library cell that is actually instantiated in the current design. Unused library cells are automatically ignored.

items: get_lib_cells returns all library (master) cells available in the loaded technology library.
foreach_in_collection iterates through each library cell one by one.
items.html: get_lib_cells returns all library (master) cells available in the loaded technology library.foreach_in_collection iterates through each library cell one by one.

items: get_attribute $lib_cell name gets the current library cell name.
get_cells -physical_context retrieves all cell instances from the physical database.
ref_name compares each instance's reference library cell with the current library cell.
sizeof_collection counts the matching instances.
items.html: get_attribute $lib_cell name gets the current library cell name.get_cells -physical_context retrieves all cell instances from the physical database.ref_name compares each instance's reference library cell with the current library cell.sizeof_collection counts the matching instances.

items.text: Checks whether the library cell is used in the design. If the count is zero, it is skipped.
items.html: Checks whether the library cell is used in the design. If the count is zero, it is skipped.

items.text: Prints the library cell name and its usage count.
items.html: Prints the library cell name and its usage count.

items: Uses optimized database filtering.
Avoids nested loops.
Faster for large SoC designs.
Commonly used in ICC2 and Fusion Compiler.
items.html: Uses optimized database filtering.Avoids nested loops.Faster for large SoC designs.Commonly used in ICC2 and Fusion Compiler.

items.text: Q1. What is the difference between get_lib_cells and get_cells?
items.html: Q1. What is the difference between get_lib_cells and get_cells?

items: get_lib_cells returns library definitions.
get_cells returns instantiated cells in the design.
items.html: get_lib_cells returns library definitions.get_cells returns instantiated cells in the design.
Q2. Why is ref_name used?

items: It identifies the library cell from which an instance is created.
items.html: It identifies the library cell from which an instance is created.

items.text: Q3. Why use sizeof_collection instead of llength?
items.html: Q3. Why use sizeof_collection instead of llength?

items: Synopsys commands return collection objects, which are counted using sizeof_collection.
items.html: Synopsys commands return collection objects, which are counted using sizeof_collection.
Q4. Why use -physical_context?

items: It queries the physical implementation database instead of only the logical database.
items.html: It queries the physical implementation database instead of only the logical database.

items.text: This is the preferred industrial method because it is simple, efficient, scalable, and widely used in PD/STA automation scripts.
items.html: This is the preferred industrial method because it is simple, efficient, scalable, and widely used in PD/STA automation scripts.

title: Script 2: Print Nets with High Fanout

items.text: This script finds all nets whose fanout is greater than or equal to a specified threshold (16 in this example) and prints the net name along with its fanout count.
items.html: This script finds all nets whose fanout is greater than or equal to a specified threshold (16 in this example) and prints the net name along with its fanout count.

items: get_nets returns all nets available in the current design.
foreach_in_collection processes one net at a time.
-physical_context queries the physical implementation database.
items.html: get_nets returns all nets available in the current design.foreach_in_collection processes one net at a time.-physical_context queries the physical implementation database.

items: get_pins -of_objects $net gets all pins connected to the current net.
-filter "direction == in" selects only the input (load) pins.
sizeof_collection counts the number of input pins, which is the fanout.
items.html: get_pins -of_objects $net gets all pins connected to the current net.-filter "direction == in" selects only the input (load) pins.sizeof_collection counts the number of input pins, which is the fanout.

items.text: Checks whether the fanout is greater than or equal to the specified threshold. Only high-fanout nets are reported.
items.html: Checks whether the fanout is greater than or equal to the specified threshold. Only high-fanout nets are reported.

items.text: Prints the net name and its fanout count.
items.html: Prints the net name and its fanout count.

items.text: Fanout is the number of load (input) pins driven by a single output pin.
items.html: Fanout is the number of load (input) pins driven by a single output pin.

items: Fanout is the number of input (load) pins driven by a single output pin.
items.html: Fanout is the number of input (load) pins driven by a single output pin.

items.text: Q2. Why count only input pins?
items.html: Q2. Why count only input pins?

items: Because fanout is defined by the number of loads, not the driver.
items.html: Because fanout is defined by the number of loads, not the driver.
Q3. Why use direction == in?

items: To exclude the driver pin and count only receiving pins.
items.html: To exclude the driver pin and count only receiving pins.
Q4. Why use sizeof_collection?

items: Because Synopsys commands return collection objects, not TCL lists.
items.html: Because Synopsys commands return collection objects, not TCL lists.
Q5. Why use -physical_context?

items: To access the physical implementation database used in Physical Design.
items.html: To access the physical implementation database used in Physical Design.

items.text: This is the preferred industrial approach because it uses optimized database queries, is efficient for large designs, and is widely used in PD, STA, CTS, and ECO automation.
items.html: This is the preferred industrial approach because it uses optimized database queries, is efficient for large designs, and is widely used in PD, STA, CTS, and ECO automation.

title: Script 3: Find the Most Used and Least Used Library Cells

items.text: This script identifies the most frequently used library cell in the design and also prints the library cells that are used only once.
items.html: This script identifies the most frequently used library cell in the design and also prints the library cells that are used only once.

items: max_count stores the highest usage count found so far.
most_used_cell stores the name of the library cell with the highest usage.

items.text: Q1. Why is max_count initialized to 0?
items.html: Q1. Why is max_count initialized to 0?

items: To ensure the first used library cell becomes the initial maximum.
items.html: To ensure the first used library cell becomes the initial maximum.
Q2. Why check cell_count > 0?

items: To ignore unused library cells.
items.html: To ignore unused library cells.

items.text: Q3. Why print cells with cell_count == 1?
items.html: Q3. Why print cells with cell_count == 1?

items: These are the least-used library cells (used only once), which may be useful for optimization or analysis.
items.html: These are the least-used library cells (used only once), which may be useful for optimization or analysis.

items.text: Q4. How is the most used library cell identified?
items.html: Q4. How is the most used library cell identified?

items: By continuously comparing each library cell's count with the current maximum and updating it when a larger value is found.
items.html: By continuously comparing each library cell's count with the current maximum and updating it when a larger value is found.

items.text: Q5. Can this script be modified to find the Top 10 most used library cells?
items.html: Q5. Can this script be modified to find the Top 10 most used library cells?

items: Yes. Store the cell names and counts in a list or dictionary, sort them by count in descending order, and print the top entries.
items.html: Yes. Store the cell names and counts in a list or dictionary, sort them by count in descending order, and print the top entries.

items.text: This is an efficient approach because it uses optimized database filtering (ref_name) instead of nested loops. It is suitable for library usage reports, design analysis, and PD/STA automation scripts.
items.html: This is an efficient approach because it uses optimized database filtering (ref_name) instead of nested loops. It is suitable for library usage reports, design analysis, and PD/STA automation scripts.

title: Script 5: Update Timing Constraints for All Scenarios

items.text: This script iterates through all timing scenarios, removes existing timing constraints (input delay and clock uncertainty), and performs a full timing update.
items.html: This script iterates through all timing scenarios, removes existing timing constraints (input delay and clock uncertainty), and performs a full timing update.

items.text: Loops through all timing scenarios (MMMC scenarios).
items.html: Loops through all timing scenarios (MMMC scenarios).

items.text: Sets the current scenario so that all subsequent commands are applied to that scenario.
items.html: Sets the current scenario so that all subsequent commands are applied to that scenario.

items.text: Removes the existing input delay constraint from the specified input port.
items.html: Removes the existing input delay constraint from the specified input port.

items.text: Removes the clock uncertainty value from the specified clock.
items.html: Removes the clock uncertainty value from the specified clock.

items.text: Performs a full timing analysis after updating the timing constraints.
items.html: Performs a full timing analysis after updating the timing constraints.

Constraint modification

items: To apply timing constraints to the selected scenario.

Q2. Why use update_timing -full?

items: To recalculate timing after modifying constraints.
items.html: To recalculate timing after modifying constraints.
Q3. What is clock uncertainty?

items: It represents clock variation due to skew and jitter and is considered during STA.
items.html: It represents clock variation due to skew and jitter and is considered during STA.

title: Script 6: Rename Combinational Cells Based on Input Port

title: Script 9: Generate Port Timing Summary Report

items.text: This script generates a report for all input and output ports by extracting important timing information such as input/output delay, delay at the port, data arrival time, and actual delay.
items.html: This script generates a report for all input and output ports by extracting important timing information such as input/output delay, delay at the port, data arrival time, and actual delay.
puts $fh "Port Name\tOutput Delay\tData Arrival\tActual Delay"
puts $fh "Port Name\tInput Delay\tData Arrival\tActual Delay"

title: Script 10: Apply Non-Default Routing (NDR) to Selected Nets

items.text: This script identifies nets connected to the selected ports and applies a Non-Default Routing (NDR) rule by specifying routing layers and routing constraints.
items.html: This script identifies nets connected to the selected ports and applies a Non-Default Routing (NDR) rule by specifying routing layers and routing constraints.

- Apply NDR to critical nets
- Improve timing on critical paths

items.text: - NDR (Non-Default Routing) is a custom routing rule that uses different wire width, spacing, or routing layers than the default routing rules.
items.html: - NDR (Non-Default Routing) is a custom routing rule that uses different wire width, spacing, or routing layers than the default routing rules.

items.text: **Q2. Why is NDR applied to critical nets?**
items.html: **Q2. Why is NDR applied to critical nets?**

items.text: - To improve timing, reduce delay, minimize crosstalk, and enhance signal integrity.
items.html: - To improve timing, reduce delay, minimize crosstalk, and enhance signal integrity.
**Q3. Why use `all_fanout` before applying NDR?**

items.text: - To identify all nets driven from the selected port so the routing rule can be applied to every connected net.
items.html: - To identify all nets driven from the selected port so the routing rule can be applied to every connected net.

title: Script 11: Manual Routing of Selected Fanout Nets Using Routing Rule

items.text: This script identifies the first-level fanout cells connected to a specified input port, collects selected nets associated with those cells, applies a custom routing rule, and performs ECO routing. It is commonly used for manual routing control, signal integrity improvement, and critical-net routing optimization.
items.html: This script identifies the first-level fanout cells connected to a specified input port, collects selected nets associated with those cells, applies a custom routing rule, and performs ECO routing. It is commonly used for manual routing control, signal integrity improvement, and critical-net routing optimization.

title: Script 12: Create Hard Bounds for Register-to-Output Timing Paths

items.text: This script identifies the endpoint register driving selected output ports, extracts the register dimensions and output port location, and creates hard placement bounds around the registers. Such bounds are commonly used to control placement near output interfaces and improve Reg-to-Out timing closure.

Such bounds are commonly used to control placement near output interfaces and improve Reg-to-Out timing closure.

title: Script 13: Remove Existing Buffers and Insert Repeaters on Port Fanout Nets

items.text: This script identifies timing paths originating from selected ports, removes existing non-sequential buffering cells on those paths, and inserts new repeaters on the associated nets. It is commonly used during ECO implementation to clean up buffering structures and improve transition, capacitance, and timing on critical paths.
items.html: This script identifies timing paths originating from selected ports, removes existing non-sequential buffering cells on those paths, and inserts new repeaters on the associated nets. It is commonly used during ECO implementation to clean up buffering structures and improve transition, capacitance, and timing on critical paths.

title: Script 1: Automated Hold Violation Fixing Using Bottleneck Analysis

It is typically used during ECO timing closure to resolve hold violations while minimizing setup timing impact.

title: Script 5: Flip-Flop Bypass Using Net Reconnection

items.text: This script bypasses a selected flip-flop by disconnecting its input/output connectivity and directly connecting the launch register output to the capture logic input. It is commonly used during ECO implementation, logic simplification, timing experiments, and functional debug activities.
items.html: This script bypasses a selected flip-flop by disconnecting its input/output connectivity and directly connecting the launch register output to the capture logic input. It is commonly used during ECO implementation, logic simplification, timing experiments, and functional debug activities.

title: Script 6: Generate Clock Skew Histogram Report

items.text: This script collects clock skew values for all sequential cells, stores the skew data into a report file, and categorizes the skew values into predefined ranges. It is commonly used for CTS quality analysis, clock-tree debugging, skew distribution studies, and signoff timing assessment.
items.html: This script collects clock skew values for all sequential cells, stores the skew data into a report file, and categorizes the skew values into predefined ranges. It is commonly used for CTS quality analysis, clock-tree debugging, skew distribution studies, and signoff timing assessment.

items.text: Answer: It helps identify skew distribution and clock tree quality across the design.

title: Script 7: Identify Long Nets and Insert Antenna Protection Diodes

items.text: This script calculates routed net lengths using Innovus database commands, identifies nets exceeding a specified length threshold, and automatically inserts antenna protection diodes on connected pins. It is useful for antenna prevention, long-net analysis, routing quality checks, and post-route ECO automation.
items.html: This script calculates routed net lengths using Innovus database commands, identifies nets exceeding a specified length threshold, and automatically inserts antenna protection diodes on connected pins. It is useful for antenna prevention, long-net analysis, routing quality checks, and post-route ECO automation.

items.text: Why are antenna diodes inserted on long nets?
items.html: Why are antenna diodes inserted on long nets?

items.text: Answer: To protect gate oxide from charge accumulation during fabrication.

items.text: What does dbGet retrieve in Innovus?
items.html: What does dbGet retrieve in Innovus?

items.text: Answer: It accesses physical database information such as nets, instances, routing, and geometry.

items.text: Why calculate total routed length instead of using a single wire segment?
items.html: Why calculate total routed length instead of using a single wire segment?

items.text: Answer: A net can contain multiple routed segments, and total length determines its actual routing impact.
